symbols: arrows piercing the body — the instruments of martyrdom and the attacks of the world upon faith
angel descending with crown — divine reward and the crown of life promised to the faithful
tree to which Sebastian is tied — echoes of the cross and the tree of life
near-nudity of the saint — vulnerability, innocence, and stripping away of worldly identity
upward gaze of the martyr — unwavering focus on heavenly reward amid earthly torment
archers drawing bows — the hostility of imperial Rome toward Christian faith
description: A muscular young man is tied to a tree at the left of the composition, his torso exposed and his body pierced by arrows. He gazes upward toward a winged angel descending from the upper right, who carries what appears to be a palm or crown. To the right, a group of soldiers and archers in colorful garments draw bows and aim at the bound figure. Additional figures crowd the middle ground amid a wooded landscape. The sky above is dramatic with golden light breaking through clouds.
theological_description: The Martyrdom of Saint Sebastian depicts one of the most celebrated of early Christian martyrs. According to tradition, Sebastian was a Roman soldier and secret Christian who was sentenced to death by Emperor Diocletian. Tied to a stake, he was shot with arrows but survived, only to be beaten to death later. While Sebastian is not a biblical figure, his martyrdom powerfully illustrates New Testament teachings about faithful witness unto death. Jesus warned his disciples: 'They will deliver you up to tribulation and put you to death, and you will be hated by all nations for my name's sake' (Matthew 24:9). The angel descending with the crown fulfills the promise of Revelation 2:10: 'Be faithful unto death, and I will give you the crown of life.' Sebastian's bound, arrow-pierced body has long been seen as a type of Christ's own passion — the innocent one suffering at the hands of worldly power. Paul's testimony resonates here: 'I have fought the good fight, I have finished the race, I have kept the faith. Henceforth there is laid up for me the crown of righteousness' (2 Timothy 4:7-8). The scene testifies to the blood of the martyrs as the seed of the Church.

scripture_refs.reason: The promise of the crown of life to those faithful unto death is visually depicted by the angel bearing a crown to the dying martyr
Jesus's prophecy that his followers would be hated and put to death is fulfilled in Sebastian's execution
Paul's declaration of having fought the good fight and awaiting the crown of righteousness parallels Sebastian's faithful martyrdom
Paul's quotation that believers are killed all day long as sheep for slaughter yet are more than conquerors through Christ
Stephen's martyrdom — the first Christian martyr gazing heavenward as he dies — establishes the pattern Sebastian follows
